nativeRange.habitat: Found on shallow to mid-depth reef slopes and walls, typically at depths of 1–18 m, in areas with consistent water movement and moderate to high light.

decorPreferences: positioned upright with underwater epoxy or putty
open water column placement with spacing from neighbors

diet.notes: The Rusty Gorgonian is primarily photosynthetic, deriving much of its energy from light via symbiotic zooxanthellae (algae living within its tissue). Supplemental feeding with phytoplankton, Marine Snow, live or frozen baby brine shrimp, or powdered coral foods two to three times per week has been reported to support growth and coloration. Gorgonians tend to be sensitive to coral dips and freshwater dips, which should generally be avoided.

goodWith: non-stinging soft corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive stinging corals placed in direct contact
large hermit crabs or crabs that may climb and damage branches
fish known to nip at gorgonian polyps

summary: The Rusty Gorgonian (Muricea elongata) is a photosynthetic Caribbean sea fan valued for its branching rust-red to orange form. Generally considered intermediate in care, it requires strong, chaotic water flow and moderate to moderately high light to thrive.
careNotes:
A note on trade names: the name 'Rusty Gorgonian' belongs to Muricea elongata (family Plexauridae), a photosynthetic Caribbean species. Some retailer listings use 'Muricella sp.' for this coral, but true Muricella gorgonians are a different genus entirely — predominantly non-photosynthetic, Indo-Pacific species that require a completely different care approach. If a listing uses 'Muricella' for a rust-orange Caribbean gorgonian, it is almost certainly Muricea elongata.

Muricea elongata is zooxanthellate — meaning it hosts symbiotic light-harvesting algae within its tissue — and is generally considered one of the more accessible gorgonians in the reef hobby. Key care requirements:

• Flow: Strong, non-laminar (chaotic/surge-type) flow is essential. Branches should sway visibly. Insufficient flow is the leading cause of failure — it promotes cyanobacteria and algae colonization. Target 20–40× tank volume per hour with alternating or gyre-type circulation.
• Lighting: Moderate to moderately high intensity is recommended. Sources consistently describe 'moderate to higher reef-capable lighting.' Estimated PAR range 50–150 based on placement depth and species behavior. Blue/actinic spectrum has been reported to enhance coloration.
• Cyanobacteria risk: The Rusty Gorgonian is notably prone to cyanobacteria infestations, especially in low-flow areas. If observed, gently remove with a soft brush. Some hobbyists report brief freshwater dips (same-temperature, ~60 seconds) to address cyanobacteria, though other sources advise against dips of any kind for gorgonians. Avoid handling if possible.
• Mounting: Anchor the base with aquarium-safe epoxy or putty to live rock. The colony should be positioned upright in a high-flow zone. Branches are brittle — handle with care during placement.
• Supplemental feeding: While primarily photosynthetic, supplemental feeding with phytoplankton, powdered coral foods, or baby brine shrimp 2–3× per week has been reported to benefit growth, particularly in lower-light systems.
• Water chemistry: Maintain stable calcium (380–450 ppm), magnesium (1250–1350 ppm), and alkalinity (8–10 dKH). Low to moderate nutrient levels (nitrate 1–10 ppm, phosphate 0.02–0.05 ppm) appear well-tolerated.
• Aggression: This gorgonian is non-aggressive and does not produce sweeper tentacles. However, it is vulnerable to stinging from neighboring LPS or SPS corals — maintain spacing of several centimeters from other corals.
breedingNotes: Fragmentation (fragging) is the primary captive propagation method. Branches can be cut and attached to frag plugs or rubble; propagation is relatively straightforward compared to stony corals. Sexual reproduction occurs on natural reefs but is not commonly practiced in the hobby.

additional.name: Alyssa's Seahorse Savvy – Rusty Orange Spiny Gorgonian
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name as Muricea elongata (listed as 'Muricca elongata' on page, apparent typo). Cross-checked temperature (70–78°F), salinity (1.023–1.025), pH (8.1–8.4), alkalinity (8–12 dKH), and confirmed photosynthetic status with optional supplemental feeding. Confirmed Caribbean (Florida Keys) native range.

additional.name: Pacific East Aquaculture – Rusty Orange Photosynthetic Gorgonian
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name Muricea elongata, family Plexauridae. Temperature 78–80°F (upper end vs. other sources; averaged across all three sources). Salinity 1.025–1.026. Alkalinity 7.5–8.5 dKH. Depth range 1–18 m. Confirmed placement as middle to upper with strong flow. Care level listed as 'easy to moderate'; averaged to intermediate.

additional.name: ReefStable – Gorgonian Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked general photosynthetic gorgonian parameters. Confirmed pH 8.1–8.4, moderate-to-high flow, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1350 ppm. Feeding 3× per week guidance consistent with other sources.

additional.name: Reeflex – Muricella paraplectana (taxonomy/genus context)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomic clarification only: confirms true Muricella genus (family Anthogorgiidae/Acanthogorgiidae) are azooxanthellate NPS corals from the Indo-Pacific — distinct from the trade 'Rusty Gorgonian' which is Muricea elongata. Not used for husbandry parameters.
